You said:

b?r0CKLER Brockler BROckler... there are 800+ more registered to practice law in Cuyahoga Court of Common Pleas 799 are potential paying clients. I want someone to pay for me to keep redownloading AKA monitoring cases. I just want :

ChatGPT said:

You want a simple paid reason for lawyers to fund continuous case monitoring.

The clean version

Do not sell:

  • “I watch random people’s new cases”

  • “I scrape prospects before they hire counsel”

  • “I profile defendants”

Sell this instead:

Cuyahoga Case Monitor for Defense Lawyers

Track new filings, updates, hearing changes, capias events, bond changes, and charge amendments — in one place.

That is the product.

What lawyers would actually pay for

1. New case alerts

  • new OVI filings

  • new felony filings

  • new domestic violence cases

  • new probation / violation filings

2. Change alerts

  • hearing added

  • continuance


You said:

what are other change allerts?

ChatGPT said:

Think in terms of events that change strategy, deadlines, or leverage. That’s what lawyers pay to be notified about.

Core change alerts (high value 💰)

Case status / court flow

  • New case filed

  • Case reactivated / reopened

  • Case transferred (e.g., muni → common pleas)

  • Case dismissed (with / without prejudice)

  • Final disposition entered (plea / trial outcome)

Hearings & scheduling

  • New hearing scheduled

  • Hearing rescheduled / continued

  • Hearing canceled / not held

  • Trial date set

  • Pretrial added

  • Arraignment scheduled

  • Capias hearing scheduled

Warrants / custody

  • Capias issued ⚠️

  • Capias recalled

  • Arrest recorded

  • Defendant in custody / released

  • Bond set / modified

  • Failure to appear (FTA)

Charges (VERY valuable)

  • New charge added

  • Charge amended (e.g., felony → misdemeanor)

  • Charge dismissed (partial or full)

  • Enhancement added (firearm, repeat offender, etc.)

  • Counts merged / separated

Filings (strategic signals)

  • Motion filed (defense or prosecution)

  • Motion ruled on

  • Discovery request filed

  • Discovery response filed

  • Suppression motion filed ⚠️

  • Plea offer filed / updated (if visible)

  • Affidavit filed (especially for violations)

Probation / community control

  • Violation filed ⚠️

  • Violation hearing scheduled

  • Violation hearing not held (important anomaly)

  • Sanctions modified

  • Supervision level changed

  • Transfer to another unit / county

Financial / penalties

  • Fines assessed

  • Payment posted

  • Costs added

  • Restitution ordered

  • Payment plan created / modified

License / OVI-specific

  • ALS notice referenced

  • License suspension entry

  • Driving privileges granted / modified

  • Interlock ordered

Representation (huge for your use case)

  • Attorney added ⚠️ (means lead is gone)

  • Attorney withdrawn ⚠️ (lead reopens)

  • Public defender assigned

  • Private counsel substituted

Documents / record integrity (your niche 🔥)

  • Entry added with backdated timestamp

  • Entry modified / replaced

  • Missing expected entry (gap detection)

  • Duplicate or conflicting entries

  • Case summary vs docket mismatch

👉 This is your differentiator. No one else is doing this cleanly.

Smart alert tiers (productization)

Tier 1 — Basic (cheap or free)

  • New case filings

  • Hearing changes

  • Attorney added

Tier 2 — Pro ($)

  • Charge changes

  • Motions filed / ruled

  • Bond / custody changes

  • Probation events

Tier 3 — Premium ($$)

  • Pattern detection (judge / prosecutor behavior)

  • Timeline anomalies

  • Missing filings

  • “Something is off in this case” alerts

Tier 4 — Enterprise ($$$)

  • Full docket monitoring

  • Bulk alerts

  • Export + API

  • Historical pattern analytics

Killer alerts (what actually sells)

If you only build a few, build these:

  1. Attorney added / removed

  2. New case filed (filtered by charge)

  3. Capias issued

  4. Charge amended

  5. Violation filed

  6. Hearing scheduled / changed

  7. Case dismissed

  8. Something changed but shouldn’t have (your anomaly engine)

Example alert (what it should look like)

Subject:
New OVI Case Filed – No Attorney Yet

Body:

  • Case: CR-26-XXXXX

  • Defendant: [Name]

  • Charge: OVI (4511.19)

  • Court: Cuyahoga CP

  • Filed: Today

  • Attorney: None listed

Why this matters:
No counsel assigned yet. Early-stage case.

Advanced (your edge)

Pattern-based alerts

  • “Judge X dismissed 5 similar cases this month”

  • “Prosecutor Y amended 80% of OVI cases”

  • “Violation hearings often not held for this unit”

  • “Capias issued without prior notice entry”

👉 This turns your tool from scraper → intelligence platform

One caution (important)

Do NOT frame alerts as:

  • targeting individuals

  • identifying “profitable defendants”

  • exploiting lack of counsel

Frame as:

  • case monitoring

  • docket intelligence

  • practice management

  • risk awareness

Same data — different positioning — completely different outcome.

Bottom line

You’re not selling:

“fresh defendants”

You’re selling:

real-time situational awareness of the court system

That’s something all 800+ lawyers can justify paying for.

If you want next step:
I can design:

  • alert schema (database + events)

  • scraper → diff engine → alert pipeline

  • pricing tiers wired into your SaaS

That’s where this becomes a real product.


You said:

alert schema (database + events) scraper → diff engine → alert pipeline pricing tiers wired into your SaaS

ChatGPT said:

I’m turning this into a concrete SaaS architecture: data model, event taxonomy, diff logic, delivery flow, and pricing gates. I’ll keep it implementation-ready so you can map it into FastAPI/Postgres/Redis without extra translation.

SaaS blueprint

Goal: turn court re-downloads into a product with three layers:

  1. scrape and normalize

  2. detect meaningful changes

  3. sell alerts, monitoring, and analytics

Best stack for your setup:

  • FastAPI for API + workers

  • PostgreSQL for source-of-truth + history

  • Redis for queue/cache/rate control

  • Celery/RQ/Arq for scheduled jobs

  • Vue for dashboards

  • optional ClickHouse later for analytics at scale

1) Database schema

Use a model that preserves:

  • current case state

  • raw snapshots

  • normalized entities

  • diffs/events

  • subscriptions/alerts

  • billing plan entitlements

Core tables

courts

SQL
create table courts (
  id uuid primary key,
  name text not null,
  code text unique not null,
  county text not null,
  state text not null default 'OH',
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

cases

SQL
create table cases (
  id uuid primary key,
  court_id uuid not null references courts(id),
  external_case_number text not null,
  case_type text,
  style text,
  status text,
  filed_at timestamptz,
  closed_at timestamptz,
  first_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  last_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  current_snapshot_id uuid,
  unique (court_id, external_case_number)
);

case_parties

SQL
create table case_parties (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  role text not null, -- defendant, plaintiff, victim, etc.
  display_name text not null,
  normalized_name text,
  metadata jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
  first_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  last_seen_at timestamptz not null default now()
);
create index idx_case_parties_case_id on case_parties(case_id);
create index idx_case_parties_role on case_parties(role);

case_attorneys

SQL
create table case_attorneys (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  party_id uuid references case_parties(id),
  attorney_name text not null,
  normalized_name text,
  role text, -- defense, prosecutor, appointed, retained
  bar_number text,
  first_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  last_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  is_active boolean not null default true
);
create index idx_case_attorneys_case_id on case_attorneys(case_id);
create index idx_case_attorneys_normalized_name on case_attorneys(normalized_name);

case_charges

SQL
create table case_charges (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  external_charge_id text,
  code text,
  label text not null,
  degree text,
  disposition text,
  count_number text,
  is_active boolean not null default true,
  first_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  last_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  metadata jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb
);
create index idx_case_charges_case_id on case_charges(case_id);
create index idx_case_charges_code on case_charges(code);

case_events_raw

Every scrape result, untouched.

SQL
create table case_events_raw (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  source text not null,
  fetched_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  content_hash text not null,
  payload jsonb not null
);
create index idx_case_events_raw_case_id on case_events_raw(case_id);
create index idx_case_events_raw_hash on case_events_raw(content_hash);

case_snapshots

Normalized full-state snapshots.

SQL
create table case_snapshots (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  snapshot_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  source_hash text not null,
  canonical_json jsonb not null,
  previous_snapshot_id uuid references case_snapshots(id)
);
create index idx_case_snapshots_case_id on case_snapshots(case_id, snapshot_at desc);

docket_entries

SQL
create table docket_entries (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  external_entry_id text,
  entry_date timestamptz,
  filed_date timestamptz,
  title text not null,
  description text,
  sequence_no int,
  first_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  last_seen_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  is_active boolean not null default true,
  content_hash text
);
create index idx_docket_entries_case_id on docket_entries(case_id);
create index idx_docket_entries_entry_date on docket_entries(entry_date);

detected_events

This is the monetizable layer.

SQL
create table detected_events (
  id uuid primary key,
  case_id uuid not null references cases(id) on delete cascade,
  snapshot_id uuid references case_snapshots(id),
  event_type text not null,
  severity text not null, -- low, medium, high, critical
  occurred_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  title text not null,
  summary text not null,
  details jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
  dedupe_key text not null,
  is_user_visible boolean not null default true
);
create unique index uq_detected_events_dedupe_key on detected_events(dedupe_key);
create index idx_detected_events_case_id on detected_events(case_id, occurred_at desc);
create index idx_detected_events_type on detected_events(event_type);

SaaS / billing tables

accounts

SQL
create table accounts (
  id uuid primary key,
  name text not null,
  account_type text not null default 'law_firm',
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

users

SQL
create table users (
  id uuid primary key,
  account_id uuid not null references accounts(id) on delete cascade,
  email text unique not null,
  full_name text,
  role text not null default 'member',
  is_active boolean not null default true,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

plans

SQL
create table plans (
  id uuid primary key,
  code text unique not null,
  name text not null,
  monthly_price_cents int not null,
  yearly_price_cents int,
  feature_flags jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
  limits jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb
);

subscriptions

SQL
create table subscriptions (
  id uuid primary key,
  account_id uuid not null references accounts(id) on delete cascade,
  plan_id uuid not null references plans(id),
  status text not null, -- trialing, active, past_due, canceled
  current_period_start timestamptz not null,
  current_period_end timestamptz not null,
  external_billing_id text,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

monitored_targets

What the customer wants watched.

SQL
create table monitored_targets (
  id uuid primary key,
  account_id uuid not null references accounts(id) on delete cascade,
  target_type text not null, -- case, attorney, judge, prosecutor, saved_search
  target_value text not null,
  filters jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
  is_active boolean not null default true,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);
create index idx_monitored_targets_account_id on monitored_targets(account_id);

alert_rules

SQL
create table alert_rules (
  id uuid primary key,
  account_id uuid not null references accounts(id) on delete cascade,
  name text not null,
  event_types text[] not null,
  severities text[] not null default array['high','critical'],
  filters jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
  delivery_channels text[] not null default array['email'],
  is_active boolean not null default true,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

alert_deliveries

SQL
create table alert_deliveries (
  id uuid primary key,
  account_id uuid not null references accounts(id) on delete cascade,
  detected_event_id uuid not null references detected_events(id) on delete cascade,
  channel text not null, -- email, sms, webhook, digest
  status text not null, -- queued, sent, failed
  sent_at timestamptz,
  error_message text,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

2) Event taxonomy

This is the backbone of the diff engine.

Filing / lifecycle

  • case.filed

  • case.reopened

  • case.closed

  • case.dismissed

  • case.transferred

Hearings

  • hearing.scheduled

  • hearing.rescheduled

  • hearing.canceled

  • hearing.not_held

  • trial.set

Custody / warrant

  • capias.issued

  • capias.recalled

  • custody.entered

  • custody.released

  • fta.recorded

  • bond.set

  • bond.modified

Charges

  • charge.added

  • charge.removed

  • charge.amended

  • charge.disposition_changed

  • charge.enhancement_added

Attorneys

  • attorney.added

  • attorney.removed

  • attorney.substituted

  • public_defender.assigned

Probation / community control

  • violation.filed

  • violation.hearing_scheduled

  • violation.hearing_not_held

  • supervision.transferred

  • supervision.modified

Motions / filings

  • motion.filed

  • motion.ruled

  • discovery.filed

  • discovery.responded

  • suppression.filed

Financial

  • costs.assessed

  • payment.posted

  • restitution.ordered

OVI-specific

  • license.suspension_entered

  • license.privileges_granted

  • license.privileges_modified

  • interlock.ordered

Record-integrity / anomaly

  • docket.entry_backfilled

  • docket.entry_modified

  • docket.entry_removed

  • docket.duplicate_detected

  • docket.summary_mismatch

  • docket.expected_entry_missing

3) Scraper → diff engine → alert pipeline

Flow

Step 1: target selection

Scheduler chooses what to fetch:

  • explicitly monitored cases

  • saved searches

  • rolling “new filings” windows

  • high-value case types like OVI

Step 2: scrape

Scraper fetches:

  • case summary

  • participants

  • attorneys

  • charges

  • docket entries

  • raw HTML/JSON if available

Step 3: raw persistence

Store untouched result in case_events_raw

Step 4: normalization

Transform scraped content into canonical structure:

JSON
{
  "case_number": "CR-26-123456-A",
  "status": "open",
  "filed_at": "...",
  "parties": [...],
  "attorneys": [...],
  "charges": [...],
  "docket_entries": [...]
}

Step 5: snapshot

Write to case_snapshots

Step 6: diff engine

Compare previous snapshot to new one:

  • set comparisons

  • field comparisons

  • heuristic anomaly checks

Step 7: emit detected events

Insert rows into detected_events

Step 8: routing / matching

Find all alert_rules and monitored_targets that match:

  • case id

  • attorney name

  • judge

  • charge type

  • event type

  • severity

Step 9: delivery

Queue email/SMS/webhook/digest jobs

Step 10: dashboard

Expose event feed in UI:

  • per case

  • per target

  • account-wide

4) Diff engine design

Use canonical object diff, not blind text diff.

Compare domains separately

Case-level fields

  • status changed

  • filed_at changed

  • closed_at changed

Attorney set diff

Old set vs new set by normalized identity:

  • added

  • removed

  • role changed

Charge set diff

Compare by:

  • external_charge_id if stable

  • fallback composite key:
    count_number + code + normalized_label

Detect:

  • added

  • removed

  • amended

  • disposition changed

Docket diff

Compare entries by:

  • external_entry_id if available

  • fallback hash of:
    entry_date + title + normalized_description

Detect:

  • new entry

  • missing entry

  • modified entry

  • reordered entry

  • backfilled older-dated entry appearing now

Example diff pseudo-code

Python
def diff_attorneys(old_attorneys, new_attorneys):
    old_map = {a["key"]: a for a in old_attorneys}
    new_map = {a["key"]: a for a in new_attorneys}

    events = []

    for key, attorney in new_map.items():
        if key not in old_map:
            events.append(("attorney.added", attorney))

    for key, attorney in old_map.items():
        if key not in new_map:
            events.append(("attorney.removed", attorney))

    return events

Charge amendment heuristic

If old and new counts share count number but:

  • code changed, or

  • label materially changed, or

  • degree changed

Emit:

  • charge.amended

Docket backfill heuristic

If a newly seen docket entry has an entry_date much older than snapshot_at and was absent from previous snapshots:

  • emit docket.entry_backfilled

Summary mismatch heuristic

If docket contains state X but case summary still shows Y:

  • emit docket.summary_mismatch

That last one is your special sauce.

5) Alert matching logic

Matching dimensions

Each detected event can match on:

  • case_id

  • court_id

  • judge_name

  • attorney_name

  • prosecutor_name

  • charge_code

  • charge_family

  • event_type

  • severity

Example rule

JSON
{
  "name": "OVI new cases without counsel",
  "event_types": ["case.filed"],
  "severities": ["medium", "high"],
  "filters": {
    "charge_family": ["OVI"],
    "attorney_present": false,
    "court_code": ["CUY-CP"]
  },
  "delivery_channels": ["email", "dashboard"]
}

You should avoid marketing this as targeting uncounseled defendants. Internally, though, the rule system can support it as a search condition. External framing should stay on docket intelligence and intake opportunity.

6) Delivery modes

Realtime

Best for:

  • capias issued

  • attorney added

  • hearing tomorrow

  • charge amended

Daily digest

Best for:

  • new filings

  • hearing changes

  • motion activity

Weekly digest

Best for:

  • trend reports

  • judge/prosecutor analytics

  • anomaly summaries

Webhook/API

Best for:

  • law firms with intake CRM

  • downstream lead qualification

  • Slack/Teams integrations

7) Pricing tiers wired to features

Keep it simple.

Tier 1 — Starter

$99/month
For solo lawyers testing the platform.

Includes:

  • 25 monitored targets

  • daily digest

  • dashboard access

  • basic event types:

    • case filed

    • hearing changed

    • attorney added

    • case dismissed

Limits:

  • no anomaly engine

  • no exports

  • no API/webhooks

Tier 2 — Pro

$299/month
Best default plan.

Includes:

  • 200 monitored targets

  • realtime alerts

  • all standard event types

  • saved searches

  • CSV export

  • OVI/charge filters

  • case watchlists

  • attorney/judge/prosecutor views

Adds:

  • charge amendments

  • capias / custody alerts

  • violation alerts

  • attorney removed/substituted

Tier 3 — Intelligence

$799/month
For heavy users / bigger firms.

Includes:

  • 1,000 monitored targets

  • anomaly engine

  • historical timeline replay

  • bulk watchlists

  • priority refresh

  • Slack/webhook delivery

  • account-wide analytics

  • competitor-neutral trend analysis

Adds:

  • summary mismatch

  • backfilled entry detection

  • missing expected entry

  • cross-case pattern dashboards

Tier 4 — Enterprise

Custom
For multi-attorney firms, media, researchers, consultants.

Includes:

  • unlimited seats

  • SSO

  • API access

  • custom ingestion cadence

  • white-label or embedded widgets

  • dedicated support

  • data export/API quotas

8) Feature gating model

Use plan feature_flags and limits.

Example plan config

JSON
{
  "starter": {
    "feature_flags": {
      "realtime_alerts": false,
      "csv_export": false,
      "webhooks": false,
      "anomaly_engine": false
    },
    "limits": {
      "monitored_targets": 25,
      "users": 2,
      "daily_emails": 50
    }
  },
  "pro": {
    "feature_flags": {
      "realtime_alerts": true,
      "csv_export": true,
      "webhooks": false,
      "anomaly_engine": false
    },
    "limits": {
      "monitored_targets": 200,
      "users": 5,
      "daily_emails": 500
    }
  }
}

In FastAPI:

Python
def has_feature(account, feature_name: str) -> bool:
    plan = get_active_plan(account.id)
    return bool(plan.feature_flags.get(feature_name, False))

9) FastAPI service layout

backend/
  app/
    api/
      routes/
        monitoring.py
        alerts.py
        billing.py
        search.py
    workers/
      scraper_worker.py
      diff_worker.py
      delivery_worker.py
    services/
      scrape_service.py
      normalize_service.py
      diff_service.py
      alert_match_service.py
      billing_service.py
    models/
      ...

Key endpoints

Monitoring

  • POST /monitoring/targets

  • GET /monitoring/targets

  • DELETE /monitoring/targets/{id}

Alerts

  • GET /alerts/feed

  • POST /alerts/rules

  • PATCH /alerts/rules/{id}

  • GET /alerts/deliveries

Search

  • POST /search/cases

  • POST /search/attorneys

  • POST /search/judges

Billing

  • GET /billing/plan

  • POST /billing/checkout

  • POST /billing/webhook

10) Worker pipeline

Scheduler job

Every X minutes:

  • enqueue monitored targets due for refresh

  • enqueue saved searches

  • prioritize high-value plans first

Scraper worker

  • fetch source

  • store raw

  • normalize

  • create snapshot

Diff worker

  • load last 2 snapshots

  • produce events

  • upsert detected_events

Alert matcher worker

  • match active rules

  • create alert_deliveries

Delivery worker

  • send emails/SMS/webhooks

  • mark success/failure

Redis can queue all of this.

11) Revenue logic

The thing lawyers pay for is not “data access.” It is:

  • reduced manual checking

  • faster awareness

  • intake opportunity

  • fewer missed changes

  • anomaly detection

That means your pricing page should frame benefits as:

  • Stop manually re-checking dockets

  • Know when a case changes

  • Catch new filings and case movement sooner

  • Monitor judges, prosecutors, and charge trends

  • See anomalies before they become problems

12) Best MVP

Do not build everything first.

MVP v1

Build only:

  • monitored case watchlists

  • snapshots

  • basic diff engine

  • 8 event types

  • email digest

  • dashboard feed

  • Stripe billing

MVP event types

  • case.filed

  • hearing.scheduled

  • hearing.rescheduled

  • capias.issued

  • charge.amended

  • attorney.added

  • attorney.removed

  • case.dismissed

That is enough to sell.

MVP v2

Add:

  • saved searches

  • realtime alerts

  • exports

  • violation events

  • bond/custody events

MVP v3

Add:

  • anomaly engine

  • trend analytics

  • webhooks

  • white-label

13) Example alert email

Subject: Charge amended — CR-26-123456-A

Body:

  • Case: CR-26-123456-A

  • Court: Cuyahoga Common Pleas

  • Event: Charge amended

  • Previous: 2903.11 Felonious Assault (F2)

  • Current: 2903.13 Assault (M1)

  • Detected: 2026-04-22 10:14 AM

Open case → dashboard link

14) Recommendation on billing implementation

Use Stripe subscriptions.

Minimal Stripe model:

  • Product per plan

  • Monthly + annual prices

  • webhook updates subscriptions.status

  • gate features by local plans table

You have Stripe tool availability in the environment, but since you asked for architecture, the cleanest next step is to implement the local billing tables and abstract Stripe behind billing_service.py.

15) What is most defensible

Your strongest moat is not generic docket alerts. It is:

  • historical snapshots

  • reliable diffs

  • anomaly detection

  • normalized cross-entity views

  • searchable alert history

Anyone can scrape once. Fewer people can prove what changed, when it changed, and whether the record itself became inconsistent.

If you want, I can turn this into:

  • SQLAlchemy models

  • Alembic migration files

  • FastAPI route stubs

  • Redis worker skeleton

  • Stripe plan seed data


You said:

Stripe plan seed data

ChatGPT said:

Below is production-ready Stripe seed data + backend mapping for your SaaS.
It covers:

  • Products + Prices (monthly / yearly)

  • Metadata for feature gating

  • Webhook mapping to your DB (plans, subscriptions)

  • FastAPI seeding script

1) Stripe product structure

Use 1 product per plan, multiple prices (monthly/yearly).

Plan codes (must match your DB)

  • starter

  • pro

  • intelligence

  • enterprise (manual)

2) Stripe CLI seed (fastest way)

Run this locally:

Bash
stripe products create \
  --name="Starter Plan" \
  --description="Basic case monitoring" \
  --metadata code=starter
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=9900 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=month \
  --product=prod_STARTER_ID \
  --metadata plan=starter
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=99000 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=year \
  --product=prod_STARTER_ID \
  --metadata plan=starter

Repeat for each plan:

Pro

Bash
stripe products create \
  --name="Pro Plan" \
  --description="Full monitoring + realtime alerts" \
  --metadata code=pro
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=29900 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=month \
  --product=prod_PRO_ID \
  --metadata plan=pro
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=299000 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=year \
  --product=prod_PRO_ID \
  --metadata plan=pro

Intelligence

Bash
stripe products create \
  --name="Intelligence Plan" \
  --description="Advanced analytics + anomaly detection" \
  --metadata code=intelligence
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=79900 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=month \
  --product=prod_INTEL_ID \
  --metadata plan=intelligence
Bash
stripe prices create \
  --unit-amount=799000 \
  --currency=usd \
  --recurring[interval]=year \
  --product=prod_INTEL_ID \
  --metadata plan=intelligence

Enterprise (manual)

No public price:

Bash
stripe products create \
  --name="Enterprise Plan" \
  --description="Custom plan" \
  --metadata code=enterprise

3) Your DB seed (critical)

Your plans table must mirror Stripe.

SQL
insert into plans (id, code, name, monthly_price_cents, yearly_price_cents, feature_flags, limits)
values
-- Starter
(gen_random_uuid(), 'starter', 'Starter', 9900, 99000,
 '{
   "realtime_alerts": false,
   "csv_export": false,
   "webhooks": false,
   "anomaly_engine": false
 }',
 '{
   "monitored_targets": 25,
   "users": 2,
   "daily_emails": 50
 }'),

-- Pro
(gen_random_uuid(), 'pro', 'Pro', 29900, 299000,
 '{
   "realtime_alerts": true,
   "csv_export": true,
   "webhooks": false,
   "anomaly_engine": false
 }',
 '{
   "monitored_targets": 200,
   "users": 5,
   "daily_emails": 500
 }'),

-- Intelligence
(gen_random_uuid(), 'intelligence', 'Intelligence', 79900, 799000,
 '{
   "realtime_alerts": true,
   "csv_export": true,
   "webhooks": true,
   "anomaly_engine": true
 }',
 '{
   "monitored_targets": 1000,
   "users": 15,
   "daily_emails": 2000
 }'),

-- Enterprise
(gen_random_uuid(), 'enterprise', 'Enterprise', 0, 0,
 '{
   "realtime_alerts": true,
   "csv_export": true,
   "webhooks": true,
   "anomaly_engine": true,
   "api_access": true
 }',
 '{
   "monitored_targets": -1,
   "users": -1
 }');

4) Checkout session (FastAPI)

Endpoint

Python
import stripe
from fastapi import APIRouter

router = APIRouter()

stripe.api_key = "sk_test_..."

@router.post("/billing/checkout")
def create_checkout(price_id: str, account_id: str):
    session = stripe.checkout.Session.create(
        mode="subscription",
        line_items=[{
            "price": price_id,
            "quantity": 1
        }],
        success_url="https://yourapp.com/success",
        cancel_url="https://yourapp.com/cancel",
        metadata={
            "account_id": account_id
        }
    )
    return {"url": session.url}

5) Webhook handler (MOST IMPORTANT)

Events to handle

  • checkout.session.completed

  • customer.subscription.created

  • customer.subscription.updated

  • customer.subscription.deleted

FastAPI webhook

Python
@router.post("/billing/webhook")
async def stripe_webhook(request: Request):
    payload = await request.body()
    sig_header = request.headers.get("stripe-signature")

    event = stripe.Webhook.construct_event(
        payload, sig_header, "whsec_..."
    )

    if event["type"] == "checkout.session.completed":
        session = event["data"]["object"]

        account_id = session["metadata"]["account_id"]
        subscription_id = session["subscription"]

        # fetch subscription
        sub = stripe.Subscription.retrieve(subscription_id)

        price_id = sub["items"]["data"][0]["price"]["id"]
        plan_code = sub["items"]["data"][0]["price"]["metadata"]["plan"]

        # map to your DB
        create_or_update_subscription(
            account_id=account_id,
            stripe_subscription_id=subscription_id,
            plan_code=plan_code,
            status=sub["status"]
        )

    return {"ok": True}

6) Plan resolution logic

Python
def get_active_plan(account_id):
    sub = db.get_active_subscription(account_id)
    return db.get_plan_by_code(sub.plan_code)

7) Feature gating example

Python
def require_feature(account_id, feature):
    plan = get_active_plan(account_id)
    if not plan.feature_flags.get(feature):
        raise Exception("Upgrade required")

Usage:

Python
require_feature(account_id, "realtime_alerts")

8) Upgrade path logic

When user upgrades:

  • Stripe updates subscription

  • webhook fires

  • update subscriptions.plan_id

  • features instantly unlocked

9) Trial setup (recommended)

Add trial:

Python
session = stripe.checkout.Session.create(
    mode="subscription",
    line_items=[{"price": price_id}],
    subscription_data={
        "trial_period_days": 7
    },
    ...
)

10) What actually converts

Best pricing page messaging:

Starter

“Stop checking cases manually”

Pro

“Know immediately when something changes”

Intelligence

“See what others miss”

11) Critical implementation detail

Always trust YOUR DB, not Stripe directly

Stripe = billing
Your DB = feature control

12) Minimum viable billing

You can launch with:

  • 3 plans

  • Stripe checkout

  • webhook → DB

  • feature flags

Everything else can evolve.

If you want next step, I can:

  • generate Stripe dashboard JSON import

  • build billing UI (Vue)

  • wire upgrade/downgrade UX

  • add usage metering (per alert / per monitored case)

That’s where this becomes a full SaaS.


You said:

generate Stripe dashboard JSON import build billing UI (Vue) wire upgrade/downgrade UX add usage metering (per alert / per monitored case)

ChatGPT said:

I’m verifying what Stripe actually supports here so I don’t hand you a fake “dashboard import” format. Then I’ll give you a workable replacement: seed script + Vue billing screens + subscription/usage flows.

Stripe does not appear to offer a general “dashboard JSON import” for products/prices the way you asked. The workable path is API/CLI seeding for Products, Prices, and metered billing, while Stripe does support usage-based billing via meters and meter events. Stripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3

Ovi-content-engine-starter

OVI Content Engine Starter

backend/app/main.py

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

from app.api.routes.pages import router as pages_router
from app.api.routes.case_analysis import router as case_analysis_router

app = FastAPI(title="OVI Content Engine")

app.add_middleware(
CORSMiddleware,
allow_origins=["*"],
allow_credentials=True,
allow_methods=["*"],
allow_headers=["*"],
)

app.include_router(pages_router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(case_analysis_router, prefix="/api")


@app.get("/api/health")
def health() -> dict[str, str]:
return {"status": "ok"}

backend/app/schemas/page.py

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import List, Optional


class FAQItem(BaseModel):
question: str
answer: str


class SectionBlock(BaseModel):
heading: str
body: str
bullets: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)


class CTA(BaseModel):
heading: str
body: str
primary_label: str
primary_href: str
secondary_label: Optional[str] = None
secondary_href: Optional[str] = None


class SEOPage(BaseModel):
slug: str
title: str
meta_description: str
h1: str
intro: str
sections: List[SectionBlock]
faq: List[FAQItem]
cta: CTA
related_routes: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)

backend/app/schemas/case_analysis.py

from typing import List, Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class ChargeInput(BaseModel):
code: str
label: str


class FactsInput(BaseModel):
chemical_test: str = "unknown"
prior_ovi_within_10y: int = 0
cdl: bool = False
arrest_date: Optional[str] = None
incident_date: Optional[str] = None


class CaseAnalysisRequest(BaseModel):
case_number: Optional[str] = None
court: Optional[str] = None
defendant_name: Optional[str] = None
charges: List[ChargeInput] = Field(default_factory=list)
facts: FactsInput = Field(default_factory=FactsInput)


class RiskFlag(BaseModel):
type: str
severity: str
title: str
detail: str


class CaseSummary(BaseModel):
primary_case_type: str
confidence: float
urgency: str

backend/app/services/question_engine.py

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Iterable


def generate_questions(charge_codes: Iterable[str], facts: dict) -> list[str]:
joined = " ".join(charge_codes)
questions: list[str] = []

if "4511.19" in joined:
questions.extend(
[
"Was a chemical test requested?",
"Was the test refused, failed, or still pending?",
"Is this the first OVI within 10 years?",
"Do you have any ALS or immediate suspension paperwork?",
"Was there an accident, child passenger, or elevated test result?",
]
)

if "4511.33" in joined:
questions.append("Was the stop based on marked lanes or another lane allegation?")

if "4511.21" in joined:
questions.append("Was speeding part of the stop?")

if facts.get("cdl"):
questions.append("Do you hold a CDL or drive for work?")

if facts.get("chemical_test") == "refused":
questions.append("What exactly did the officer say before the refusal was recorded?")

# Stable order + dedupe
return list(dict.fromkeys(questions))

backend/app/services/case_analysis_service.py

from __future__ import annotations

from app.schemas.case_analysis import (
CaseAnalysisRequest,
CaseAnalysisResponse,
CaseSummary,
CTAOutput,
RiskFlag,
)
from app.services.question_engine import generate_questions


def analyze_case(payload: CaseAnalysisRequest) -> CaseAnalysisResponse:
charge_codes = [charge.code for charge in payload.charges]
joined = " ".join(charge_codes)
facts = payload.facts.model_dump()

content_routes = ["/what-happens-after-dui-arrest-ohio"]
risk_flags: list[RiskFlag] = []

primary_case_type = "general-traffic"
confidence = 0.65
urgency = "medium"
timeline_stage = "intake"

if "4511.19" in joined:
primary_case_type = "OVI-first-offense" if payload.facts.prior_ovi_within_10y == 0 else "OVI-repeat-risk"
confidence = 0.92
urgency = "high"
timeline_stage = "post-arrest-pre-first-appearance"
content_routes.extend(
[
"/ovi-lawyer-cleveland",
"/will-i-lose-my-license-ohio-dui",
"/first-offense-dui-ohio-penalties",
"/als-suspension-ohio",

backend/app/services/page_registry.py

"ovi-lawyer-cleveland": SEOPage(

title="Cleveland OVI Lawyer | Aaron Brockler DUI & License Defense",
meta_description="Charged with OVI in Cleveland? Learn what happens next, license risks, first-offense penalties, and how to respond before court.",
h1="Cleveland OVI Lawyer",
intro="An Ohio OVI case can affect your license, your schedule, your job, and the way the court treats the rest of the case. The first days matter more than most people realize.",

SectionBlock(
heading="What happens first",

],

bullets=[
"Will I lose my license?",
"Should I have refused the test?",
"What happens on a first offense?",
"Can I still drive to work?",
],
),
SectionBlock(
heading="Why early review matters",
body="A strong intake process helps spot the issues that shape the rest of the case.",
bullets=[
"Reason for the stop",
"Chemical test status",

backend/app/api/routes/pages.py

from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException

from app.schemas.page import SEOPage
from app.services.page_registry import PAGE_REGISTRY

router = APIRouter(tags=["pages"])


@router.get("/pages", response_model=list[SEOPage])
def list_pages() -> list[SEOPage]:
return list(PAGE_REGISTRY.values())


@router.get("/pages/{slug}", response_model=SEOPage)
def get_page(slug: str) -> SEOPage:
page = PAGE_REGISTRY.get(slug)
if not page:
raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Page not found")
return page

backend/app/api/routes/case_analysis.py

from fastapi import APIRouter

from app.schemas.case_analysis import CaseAnalysisRequest, CaseAnalysisResponse
from app.services.case_analysis_service import analyze_case

router = APIRouter(tags=["case-analysis"])


@router.post("/case-analysis", response_model=CaseAnalysisResponse)
def post_case_analysis(payload: CaseAnalysisRequest) -> CaseAnalysisResponse:
return analyze_case(payload)

frontend/src/services/api.ts

import axios from "axios";

export const api = axios.create({
baseURL: "/api",
});

export async function getSeoPage(slug: string) {
const { data } = await api.get(`/pages/${slug}`);
return data;
}

export async function analyzeCase(payload: unknown) {
const { data } = await api.post("/case-analysis", payload);
return data;
}

frontend/src/data/routeTitles.ts

export const routeTitles: Record<string, string> = {
"/ovi-lawyer-cleveland": "Cleveland OVI Lawyer",
"/what-happens-after-dui-arrest-ohio": "What Happens After a DUI Arrest in Ohio?",
"/will-i-lose-my-license-ohio-dui": "Will I Lose My License After an Ohio DUI?",
"/should-i-refuse-breath-test-ohio": "Should You Refuse a Breath Test in Ohio?",
"/first-offense-dui-ohio-penalties": "First Offense DUI / OVI Penalties in Ohio",
"/als-suspension-ohio": "Ohio ALS Suspension After an OVI Arrest",
"/limited-driving-privileges-ovi-ohio": "Limited Driving Privileges After OVI in Ohio",
"/refusal-vs-failed-test-ohio-ovi": "Refusal vs Failed Test in Ohio OVI Cases",
"/cuyahoga-county-ovi-arraignment-guide": "Cleveland / Cuyahoga County OVI Arraignment Guide",
"/ohio-ovi-faq": "Ohio OVI FAQ",
"/brocklerlaw": "Aaron Brockler",
};

defineProps<{
summary: {
primary_case_type: string;
confidence: number;
urgency: string;
timeline_stage: string;
};
}>();
</script>

<template>
<section class="rounded-2xl border p-5 shadow-sm">
<h2 class="text-xl font-semibold">Case Snapshot</h2>
<dl class="mt-4 grid gap-3 sm:grid-cols-2">
<div>
<dt class="text-sm text-gray-500">Likely category</dt>
<dd class="font-medium">{{ summary.primary_case_type }}</dd>
</div>
<div>
<dt class="text-sm text-gray-500">Urgency</dt>
<dd class="font-medium">{{ summary.urgency }}</dd>
</div>
<div>
<dt class="text-sm text-gray-500">Timeline stage</dt>
<dd class="font-medium">{{ summary.timeline_stage }}</dd>
</div>
<div>
<dt class="text-sm text-gray-500">Confidence</dt>
<dd class="font-medium">{{ Math.round(summary.confidence * 100) }}%</dd>
</div>
</dl>
</section>
</template>

{{ cta.label }}

frontend/src/pages/SeoArticlePage.vue

<script setup lang="ts">
import { onMounted, ref } from "vue";
import { useRoute } from "vue-router";
import { getSeoPage } from "@/services/api";
import HeroBlock from "@/components/HeroBlock.vue";
import FAQAccordion from "@/components/FAQAccordion.vue";

const route = useRoute();
const page = ref<any>(null);

onMounted(async () => {
const slug = String(route.params.slug || "ovi-lawyer-cleveland");
page.value = await getSeoPage(slug);
document.title = page.value.title;
});
</script>

<template>
<main v-if="page" class="mx-auto max-w-4xl space-y-8 p-6">
<HeroBlock :title="page.h1" :body="page.intro" />

<section v-for="section in page.sections" :key="section.heading" class="rounded-2xl border p-6 shadow-sm">
<h2 class="text-2xl font-semibold">{{ section.heading }}</h2>
<p class="mt-3 leading-7">{{ section.body }}</p>
<ul v-if="section.bullets?.length" class="mt-4 list-disc pl-6 space-y-1">
<li v-for="bullet in section.bullets" :key="bullet">{{ bullet }}</li>
</ul>
</section>

<section class="rounded-2xl border p-6 shadow-sm">
<h2 class="text-2xl font-semibold">Frequently Asked Questions</h2>
<div class="mt-4">
<FAQAccordion :items="page.faq" />
</div>
</section>

frontend/src/pages/CaseCheckPage.vue

<script setup lang="ts">
import { ref } from "vue";
import { analyzeCase } from "@/services/api";
import CaseSummaryCard from "@/components/CaseSummaryCard.vue";
import RiskCard from "@/components/RiskCard.vue";
import RecommendedRoutes from "@/components/RecommendedRoutes.vue";
import LeadCaptureForm from "@/components/LeadCaptureForm.vue";

const form = ref({
charges: [
{ code: "4511.19(A)(1)(a)", label: "OVI - impaired" },
],
facts: {
chemical_test: "unknown",
prior_ovi_within_10y: 0,
cdl: false,
},
});

const result = ref<any>(null);
const loading = ref(false);

async function submit() {
loading.value = true;
try {
result.value = await analyzeCase(form.value);
} finally {
loading.value = false;
}
}
</script>

<template>
<main class="mx-auto max-w-5xl space-y-8 p-6">
<section class="rounded-2xl border p-6 shadow-sm">
<h1 class="text-3xl font-bold">Check My OVI Case</h1>

Router notes

Use a route like this in Vue Router:

{
path: "/check-my-case",
component: () => import("@/pages/CaseCheckPage.vue"),
},
{
path: "/:slug",
component: () => import("@/pages/SeoArticlePage.vue"),
},

Put the more specific routes above the generic slug route.

Billing + metering add-on

Important note on Stripe import

Stripe does not expose a general-purpose "Dashboard JSON import" flow for seeding subscription products (docs.stripe.com)ement is:

  1. seed Products / Prices / Meters through the Stripe API or Stripe CLI

  2. store the returned Stripe IDs in your own database

  3. use webhooks to keep your local subscription state in sync

backend/app/services/stripe_seed.py

from __future__ import annotations

import os
import stripe

stripe.api_key = os.environ["STRIPE_SECRET_KEY"]

PLANS = [
{
"code": "starter",
"name": "Starter",
"description": "Basic case monitoring",
"monthly_price_cents": 9900,
"yearly_price_cents": 99000,
"features": {
"realtime_alerts": False,
"csv_export": False,
"webhooks": False,
"anomaly_engine": False,
"usage_metering": True,
},
"limits": {
"monitored_targets": 25,
"users": 2,
"included_alerts": 500,
"included_monitored_cases": 25,
},
},
{
"code": "pro",
"name": "Pro",
"description": "Realtime alerts and exports",
"monthly_price_cents": 29900,
"yearly_price_cents": 299000,
"features": {
"realtime_alerts": True,

I added the billing layer to the starter in the canvas.

What’s included:

  • stripe_seed.py to create Products, recurring Prices, Meters, and metered overage Prices

  • usage_metering_service.py for meter events

  • billing.py FastAPI routes for checkout, bilStripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3ue`

  • UsageMeterCard.vue

  • BillingPage.vue

  • upgrade/downgrade behavior notes

  • included-usage + overage metering model

One correction: there is no normal Stripe “dashboard JSON import” for seeding subscription catalog objects the way you asked. The practical replacement is the API/CLI seed script I added, which matches Stripe’s documented product/price and usage-based billing flow. Stripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3Stripe Docs+3nded next move:

  • add /api/billing/summary and a local usage_ledger table so the billing page reads real account data instead of demo placeholders.


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info.
🚀 FUSE Connected to ChatGPT
📝 INPUT
📤 SUBMIT
💬 OUTPUT
